query: causes of incomplete casting in dental and orthopedic applications

retrieved_docs.title: Craniofacial Distraction Osteogenesis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: If the fracture is not immobilized, this ossification will not complete and a fibrous union results. While this is undesirable after an accidental fracture (the basis for casting or other rigid fixation after fractures), the intentional movement of an osteotomy along a controlled vector gradually stretches this fibrous callus, which is subsequently immobilized allowing ossification. The net result is a lengthening of the bone at the site of the osteotomy. This technique has become widely used for malformations of the cranial skeleton and is used to elongate the mandible, midface, and calvarium. The advantages of the approach are its creation of strong bone in a controlled manner, resistance to relapse, and adaptive changes of the soft tissue envelope.

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Bone Immobilization -- Issues of Concern -- Types of Splints
retrieved_docs.content: Rigid splints are made out of plastic, malleable metals, wood, or other materials. They tend to be less flexible and more supportive than soft splints and are usually padded. They are typically secured to the patient via circumferentially applied adhesive, bandages, or straps. The joint above and below the injury should be immobilized as well.

retrieved_docs.title: Cracked Tooth Syndrome -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Indirect cuspal coverage in the form of a crown or onlay may also function extremely well as an external splint, prohibiting the flexure of the crack. Guthrie et al observed that most cracks extended into the cervical third of the crown. In their study, they concluded that 25 of the 28 tested cracked teeth became asymptomatic after receiving a full-coverage crown. [30] A full-coverage crown allows for a more equal distribution of occlusal forces across the affected tooth due to the resistant form of the crown. This prevents the crack's flexure and inhibits it from propagating further and causing more damage. [30] However, tooth preparation for full-coverage crowns is invasive and increases the risk of devitalization. [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Retrognathia -- Treatment Planning
retrieved_docs.content: A patient who has retrognathia from mandibular AP hypoplasia will enter the treatment planning phase of their case during the first visit with the orthodontist. The orthodontist because will plan treatment years into the future and make movement and teeth extraction decisions based on skeletal growth and facial characteristics. Once the patient is ready for surgical correction, they will be referred to an oral and maxillofacial surgeon. A full series of photos and dental models are generally taken along with radiographs and a full workup completed and discussed with the treatment team, orthodontist, and patient. At this point, the surgical options are presented to the patient, and a surgical course decided upon. The standard workup and planning involving all proposed surgeries be completed on dental models and precise movements documented. Acrylic splints are then fabricated for use in the stabilization of the teeth and bones for rigid internal fixation during surgery. More common over the last decade is virtual surgical planning, where a computed tomography (CT) scan and patient models are digitally merged, and the operation is virtually planned within 0.01 mm; final surgical splints are custom printed on a 3D printer. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Bone Immobilization -- Issues of Concern -- Types of Splints
retrieved_docs.content: Air splints are essentially large air bladders that are initially deflated and wrapped around the affected extremity. After application, they are inflated to provide rigidity through air pressure.

retrieved_docs.title: Chance Fractures -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The chance fracture is usually reduced on a Risser table by applying hyperextension to the injured thoracolumbar area. A custom-designed plaster or fiberglass is then applied. The biggest drawback is patient compliance because the orthotic device is uncomfortable. The cast is maintained for 8-12 weeks. X-rays are then obtained to determine any residual deformity, but overall, union rates are high with conservative management. An extensive rehabilitation program is necessary to regain muscle strength and mobility.

retrieved_docs.title: Splinting -- Technique or Treatment -- Splinting Technique
retrieved_docs.content: Ensure adequate analgesia before splint application to promote muscle relaxation and facilitate fracture reduction.

retrieved_docs.title: Orthopedic Implant Materials -- Issues of Concern -- Surgically Applicable Machining Properties
retrieved_docs.content: Originators of open reduction and absolutely stable fixation in Europe locally sourced many of their early implants. The relatively widely available stainless steel provided a substrate on which modern fracture care was developed. In many cases, implants were developed in very basic machining environments and built-to-spec on a case by case basis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2204
retrieved_docs.content: Other OR therapeutic procedures on musculoskeletal system is a classification term that categorizes other or therapeutic procedures on the musculoskeletal system, grouping together various interventions aimed at treating musculoskeletal conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 209
retrieved_docs.content: The category “THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS” comprises a comprehensive set of operative interventions that target the femur and adjacent structures. Child procedures include epiphyseal arrest techniques—“Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); combined distal femur, proximal tibia and fibula,” “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); distal femur,” and “Arrest, hemiepiphyseal, distal femur or proximal tibia or fibula (eg, genu varus or valgus)”—which modulate growth plates to correct deformities. Fracture‑stabilizing operations encompass “Femoral shortening with blade plate,” “Internal fixation of femur” (with and without fracture reduction), “Intramedullary nailing of femur,” “Open reduction of fracture of femur” (with or without internal fixation), and “Trochanterplasty.” Osteoplastic modifications are represented by “Osteoplasty of femur,” “Osteoplasty of femur, lengthening,” “Osteoplasty of femur, shortening,” and “Osteoplasty, femur; combined, lengthening and shortening with femoral segment transfer,” allowing alteration of femoral length and contour. Preventive and corrective measures include “Prophylactic treatment of femur,” “Repair of malunion, of femur, distal to head and neck,” and “Suture of periosteum of femur.” Prosthetic management is covered by “Replacement of prosthesis of femur with use of methyl methacrylate” and “Revision of total knee arthroplasty, all components (procedure).” Clinically, these procedures address the primary anatomical site of lower‑limb fractures, exemplified by the entity “fracture of lower limb” classified as an unspecified closed fracture of the femoral neck, underscoring the femur’s central role in diagnosis, fracture stabilization, growth modulation, length alteration, and implant revision within orthopedic care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 877
retrieved_docs.content: The late effect of fracture of multiple bones, the late consequences of fracture of the skull and facial bones, and the late effects of fracture of the femoral neck are all described as being associated with a fracture (كسر). In addition, the open reduction of an open shoulder dislocation with fracture of the greater tuberosity is identified as a direct morphological manifestation of a fracture (كسر). Together, these statements highlight that both delayed sequelae across various skeletal regions and specific surgical interventions are directly linked to the underlying presence of a fracture, emphasizing the need for comprehensive assessment and management of fracture‑related pathology in patient care.
